1: τὸν προπάτορα ἡμῶν κατὰ σάρκα (ton propatora hēmōn kata sarka) — "our forefather according to the flesh." The kata sarka qualification anticipates Paul's argument: Abraham is relevant as forefather according to the flesh only as a starting point; his real significance is as father of faith (vv11-12). The shift from fleshly descent to faith-descent is the letter's central move for defining who belongs to the people of God.
2: ἔχει καύχημα ἀλλ᾽ οὐ πρὸς θεόν (echei kauchēma all' ou pros theon) — "he has a boast, but not before God." Kauchēma (boast, ground of pride) — Paul grants the hypothetical: if Abraham were justified by works, he would have a legitimate ground of pride before other humans. But the divine court operates differently; what impresses human evaluators does not constitute righteousness before God, whose standard is being-without-boast (3:27).
3: ἐλογίσθη αὐτῷ εἰς δικαιοσύνην (elogisthē autō eis dikaiosynēn) — "it was credited to him as righteousness." The aorist passive of logizomai: a single completed act of divine reckoning. The subject is "faith" (Abraham's trust in God's promise); the object reckoned is "righteousness" — the standing before God that the faith-act receives as its divine valuation. The verb will be repeated eight times in ch4, building a sustained argument from the single Genesis text (Gen 15:6).
4: τὸ μισθὸς οὐ λογίζεται κατὰ χάριν ἀλλὰ κατὰ ὀφείλημα (to misthos ou logizetai kata charin alla kata opheilēma) — "the wage is not credited according to grace but according to what is owed." Opheilēma (debt, what is owed) — the wage-worker analogy establishes the contrast: wages are debt-obligations, not gifts. If justification were by works, God would be the debtor; but God is no one's debtor, making justification-by-works a category error about the nature of the divine-human relationship.
5: τῷ δὲ μὴ ἐργαζομένῳ πιστεύοντι δὲ ἐπὶ τὸν δικαιοῦντα τὸν ἀσεβῆ (tō de mē ergazomenō pisteuonti de epi ton dikaiounta ton asebē) — "but to the one who does not work but believes in the one who justifies the ungodly." Ton asebē (the ungodly one) is the stunning element: God justifies the ungodly, not the merely-imperfect or the almost-righteous. The one who has no works to offer is precisely the candidate for grace-reckoning. Paul's description of God as "the one who justifies the ungodly" directly inverts Exod 23:7 ("do not acquit the guilty").
6: μακαρισμὸν (makarismon) — "blessedness," the state of being blessed — the same root as the Beatitudes (makarios). David's declaration of blessedness in Ps 32:1-2 becomes Paul's second OT witness (after Gen 15:6) that right-standing before God is received, not earned. The Psalter's own voice confirms the Genesis principle.
7: ἀφέθησαν... ἐπεκαλύφθησαν (aphethēsan... epekaluphthēsan) — "have been forgiven... have been covered" — both aorist passives; the divine act of forgiveness is completed and stands. Aphiēmi (to send away, release) and epikaryptō (to cover over) are two images for the same reality: the debt is cancelled and the evidence is hidden. LXX Ps 32:1 uses these verbs; Paul imports them verbatim.
8: οὗ οὐ μὴ λογίσηται κύριος ἁμαρτίαν (hou ou mē logisētai kyrios hamartian) — "to whom the Lord will never reckon sin" — the double negative ou mē with the subjunctive is emphatic negation: under no circumstances, not ever. The future subjunctive extends the non-reckoning into perpetuity. The same reckoning-verb that credits righteousness positively here refuses to credit sin negatively — both sides of the forensic ledger are covered by grace.
9: ἐν περιτομῇ ὄντι ἢ καὶ ἐν ἀκροβυστίᾳ (en peritomē onti ē kai en akrobystia) — "to the circumcised or also to the uncircumcised?" Paul now asks when Abraham received the blessing — before or after circumcision. The temporal argument from Genesis: Gen 15:6 (faith credited) precedes Gen 17 (circumcision instituted) by approximately 14 years, making Abraham's justification happen in uncircumcision.
10: οὐκ ἐν περιτομῇ ἀλλ᾽ ἐν ἀκροβυστίᾳ — "not in circumcision but in uncircumcision" — the historical sequence is Paul's argument: the faith-reckoning happened when Abraham was still an uncircumcised Gentile. The implication is that faith-righteousness is structurally prior to covenant-rite, making it available to all who believe regardless of circumcision-status.
11: σφραγῖδα τῆς δικαιοσύνης τῆς πίστεως (sphragida tēs dikaiosynēs tēs pisteōs) — "a seal of the righteousness of the faith" — sphragis (seal) is the authentication mark that certifies a document's validity. Circumcision is the external seal that certifies the prior righteousness-by-faith; it does not produce the righteousness but certifies its existence. This makes circumcision meaningful but non-essential for the righteousness itself.
12: τοῖς στοιχοῦσιν τοῖς ἴχνεσιν τῆς ἐν ἀκροβυστίᾳ πίστεως (tois stoichousin tois ichnesin tēs en akrobystia pisteōs) — "who follow in the footsteps of the faith that our father Abraham had while still uncircumcised." Stoicheō (to march in line, follow in order) + ichnesin (footprints, tracks) — a vivid image of following someone's literal footsteps. Those who retrace Abraham's faith-path (not his circumcision-path) become his offspring.
13: κληρονόμον αὐτὸν εἶναι κόσμου (klēronomon auton einai kosmou) — "that he would be heir of the world" — klēronomos (heir, one who receives the inheritance by lot) — the promise to Abraham (land, seed, blessing) is here universalized to "the world," which Paul reads as the eschatological new-creation inheritance. The local promise to Canaan becomes the cosmic promise of the new earth; Paul reads Genesis through the lens of the prophets who expanded the Abrahamic promise to universal scope.
14: εἰ γὰρ οἱ ἐκ νόμου κληρονόμοι (ei gar hoi ek nomou klēronomoi) — "if those who are of the law are heirs" — the conditional hypothetical: if inheritance were through law-keeping, then faith would be emptied (kekenōtai, perfect passive — "has been emptied/rendered void") and the promise would be nullified (katērgētai — "rendered inoperative"). The law cannot be the mechanism of inheritance because it cannot do what it demands (3:19-20).
15: ὁ γὰρ νόμος ὀργὴν κατεργάζεται (ho gar nomos orgēn katergazetai) — "for the law brings about wrath" — the present active: law's operation produces wrath because it defines the standard that condemns its own transgressors. οὗ δὲ οὐκ ἔστιν νόμος, οὐδὲ παράβασις — "where there is no law, there is no transgression either" — parabasis (deliberate stepping-past) requires a known boundary; without the law, moral failure is hamartia (missing the mark) but not parabasis (knowing violation).
16: ἐκ πίστεως, ἵνα κατὰ χάριν (ek pisteōs, hina kata charin) — "from faith, so that according to grace" — the causal chain: faith-basis → grace-character → promise-security. The logic is: only a grace-based promise can be universally reliable, because performance-based promises fail when performance fails. βεβαίαν εἶναι τὴν ἐπαγγελίαν παντὶ τῷ σπέρματι — "that the promise might be secure to all the offspring" — bebaios (firm, guaranteed, bankable) — a commercial-legal term for a guaranteed transaction; the promise is reliable precisely because it depends on God's faithfulness, not human performance.
17: τοῦ ζωοποιοῦντος τοὺς νεκροὺς καὶ καλοῦντος τὰ μὴ ὄντα ὡς ὄντα (tou zōopoiountos tous nekrous kai kalountos ta mē onta hōs onta) — "the one who gives life to the dead and calls things that are not as though they are." Two divine attributes grounding Abraham's faith: (1) zōopoieō (give life) — applicable both to Sarah's womb and to the resurrection; (2) the creative calling of non-being into being (ta mē onta hōs onta) — the language of creatio ex nihilo. The God Abraham trusted is the Creator and Resurrector simultaneously.
18: παρ᾽ ἐλπίδα ἐπ᾽ ἐλπίδι (par' elpida ep' elpidi) — "against hope, in hope." The paradoxical phrase is precisely constructed: par' elpida (contrary to visible/calculable expectation) and ep' elpidi (grounded-on-hope = the divine promise). Abraham's faith is rational at the divine level while irrational at the human level; it trusts the God who makes promises rather than the circumstances that deny their possibility.
19: μὴ ἀσθενήσας τῇ πίστει (mē asthenēsas tē pistei) — "not weakening in faith" — the aorist participle: he did not at the decisive moment collapse in unbelief. κατενόησεν τὸ ἑαυτοῦ σῶμα ἤδη νενεκρωμένον (katenovēsen to heautou sōma ēdē nenekrōmenon) — "he considered his own body, already deadened" — nenekrōmenon: perfect passive participle of nekroō (to put to death, make corpse-like). Abraham saw the biological reality clearly; his faith was not blind optimism but clear-eyed trust in the promise-maker.
20: εἰς δὲ τὴν ἐπαγγελίαν τοῦ θεοῦ οὐ διεκρίθη τῇ ἀπιστίᾳ ἀλλ᾽ ἐνεδυναμώθη τῇ πίστει — "but regarding the promise of God he did not waver in unbelief but was strengthened in faith." Diakrinomai (to be divided, waver, doubt) — the passive implies an external force that Abraham did not yield to. Enedynamoō (to be empowered, strengthened) — the passive equally implies a divine empowerment; faith itself is a gift that enables trust in the promise. δοὺς δόξαν τῷ θεῷ — "giving glory to God" — glory-giving through faith rather than through performance is Paul's definition of true worship.
21: πεπληροφορημένος (peplērophorēmenos) — perfect passive participle of plērophoreō (to be fully persuaded, carried to fullness of conviction). The completeness of Abraham's persuasion is emphasized by the plēro- prefix and the perfect tense: he arrived at and remained in full conviction. The object: the divine ability to do what he promised (ποιῆσαι, hoper epēngeltai, dynatos ēstin).
22: διὸ καὶ ἐλογίσθη αὐτῷ εἰς δικαιοσύνην — "therefore it was also credited to him as righteousness" — the dio kai (therefore also) marks the logical consequence: the faith just described in vv17-21 is the faith that Gen 15:6 credits as righteousness. Paul's description of Abraham's faith is a theological unpacking of the Genesis statement, not a separate claim.
23: οὐκ ἐγράφη δὲ δι᾽ αὐτὸν μόνον (ouk egraphē de di' auton monon) — "but it was not written for his sake only" — the passive egraphē (it was written) is a divine passive: God wrote the Scripture for all its future readers, not merely as a record of Abraham's biography. The hermeneutical principle: OT narrative has a prospective, not merely retrospective, function.
24: τοῖς πιστεύουσιν ἐπὶ τὸν ἐγείραντα Ἰησοῦν τὸν κύριον ἡμῶν ἐκ νεκρῶν — "for those who believe in the one who raised Jesus our Lord from the dead." The resurrection of Christ is the definitive divine act that constitutes the content of the present-day faith that receives the same reckoning Abraham received. Believing in the resurrection-God is the NT form of Abraham's believing in the promise-God.
25: παρεδόθη διὰ τὰ παραπτώματα ἡμῶν καὶ ἠγέρθη διὰ τὴν δικαίωσιν ἡμῶν — "he was handed over on account of our trespasses and raised on account of our justification." The chiasm (death → trespasses; resurrection → justification) pairs the two events with their respective grounds. Egēgertai in other contexts would be perfect (4:25 uses aorist here: ēgerthē); the aorist marks the historical event. Dia with accusative can mean "on account of/for the sake of" or "because of": both meanings are simultaneously true — Christ raised because justification is accomplished and in order that justification is applied.
